FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data (2016-2020), sample of 6,749 adolescents from 21 recruitment sites across the United States

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures screen time as specific smartphone/digital media variable: self-reported hours of use on typical day including multiple-player gaming, single-player gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, browsing internet, watching/streaming videos/TV (excluding schoolwork)

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression analyses examining associations between ACE score (0-9 continuous) and screen time outcomes; adjusted models with multiple covariates reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric results reported: adjusted B = 0.44 (95% CI 0.26, 0.62), p < 0.001 for ACEs predicting hours per day screen time; youth with ≥4 ACEs averaged 2.3 more hours screen time per day vs 0 ACEs

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis - cross-sectional analysis from May 2020 COVID RRR Survey data, linear regression design

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Academic Pediatrics 2022;22:1294-1299, not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply - paper includes smartphone/screen time as DEPENDENT variable BUT predictors include individual psychological characteristics (trauma history/ACEs indicate psychological distress exposure), not solely demographic predictors. Also analyzes associations with multiple non-screen-time outcomes (physical activity).
notes: Clear inclusion - screen time is specific outcome measured and associational statistics are well-reported. Screen time measured as composite of 7 specific modalities (gaming, texting, social media, video chat, internet, streaming). ACE score is predictor ranging 0-9.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD dataset, 9,855 children from USA, ages 9-10 at baseline, with 2-year follow-up data

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures three specific screen time types: watching (TV/videos), socializing (social media/video chat), and gaming (video games), with separate time measurements for each modality

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple inferential statistical tests reported: correlations (r values), standardized beta coefficients (β) from latent change score models, mediation analyses examining effects of screen time types on intelligence change over two years

FT_IC4.evidence: Extensive numeric results: baseline correlations (r = -0.12 for watching, r = -0.10 for socializing); 2-year prospective effects (β = +0.17 for gaming, β = +0.12 for watching on intelligence); detailed coefficients and confidence intervals throughout

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis - longitudinal design following children from age 9-10 to 11-12 with intelligence measured at baseline and 2-year follow-up; latent change score modeling

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Scientific Reports 2022;12:7720, not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply - paper includes individual psychological/cognitive predictors (cogPGS for cognitive performance, SES for socioeconomic status are confounders but paper also examines genetic factors). Paper reports smartphone/screen time → intelligence outcome associations (not screen time as only DV). Screen time is predictor, intelligence is outcome.
notes: Clear inclusion - screen time variables are specific (watching, socializing, gaming), inferential tests are well-documented with numeric coefficients. Longitudinal design with clear directional associations. Uses SEM/mediation framework.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD cohort data, 11,000+ children aged 9-11 years, includes genetics, neuroimaging, and behavioral data from this large-scale USA dataset

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures screen time utilization (STU) as specific variable in both cross-sectional and longitudinal analyses; defined as time spent on screens with behavioral assessments

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple inferential statistical tests reported: cross-sectional associations between polygenic risk scores and STU, longitudinal analyses examining directional effects, mediation analysis testing white matter microstructure as mediator between ADHD polygenic risk and STU

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results reported including: correlations between FA (fractional anisotropy) values and both ADHD polygenic risk score and STU; mediation pathways showing white matter tracts link ADHD genetic risk to STU; directional and bi-directional effects described

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis - cross-sectional and longitudinal analyses examining associations between genetics, neuroimaging (white matter microstructure), ADHD symptoms, and STU

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in EBioMedicine 2022;104039, not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply - paper includes individual psychological/clinical predictor (ADHD polygenic risk score, ADHD trait/symptoms), not solely demographic predictors. Also includes neuroimaging predictor (white matter microstructure). Per E16, individual-characteristic predictors of phone/SM (like ADHD polygenic risk) → MET regardless of direction/timeframe.
notes: Clear inclusion - STU is measured as screen time outcome variable. Paper reports associations between genetic risk for ADHD and screen time, mediated through white matter microstructure. Employs cross-sectional and longitudinal designs with mediation analysis framework.
